speaker: interviewer
text: March 12, 1997. Mr. Manus, you said that there were functioning Hebrew schools in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were. They were kind of illegal, but we were going to the teacher's house in smaller groups. [PAUSES] And-- [PAUSES] but it wasn't at length that we did before. But we did take some Hebrew school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any youth organizations that were active in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really can't, uh-- can't say that we did have, no. The, uh, kibbutz we had in our town had disappeared, you know. So everything was-- a Jew couldn't do anything. You couldn't belong to any group. You couldn't-- you-- you just-- it was dormant. Everything was on a standstill.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the kibbutz program?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was before the Germans came in. We had a kibbutz in the town and they were, uh, taken in the street raids. And, uh, we had a large lumber yard. And they were working in the lumber yard and, uh, practicing army and, uh, different, uh, things, preparing them for immigration to Israel, to Palestine at that time. And usually there was, in them days-- if I'm right, I think I am-- that there was quotas. So many Jews were allowed to go to Palestine. So from that kibbutz, they were the first ones to go. So I don't know how many per year went, and they recruited others, replacements.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were not allowed to have your kibbutz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not during the Nazis, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to observe any Jewish holidays, or the Sabbath in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only in private. We observed kashrut. We observed everything. And, uh, we were, like I mentioned, we were very kosher, and, uh, fanatically, maybe, kosher, my mother was. I can give you an instance that, uh-- my mother was boiling milk and she was making hamburgers during the Nazi time. And the milk spilled over, over the hamburgers. And in the Jewish law, that's not kosher. You can't eat the hamburger if milk spills on it. But naturally things were very hard to get food in them days.

speaker: interviewee
text: So mother send me to go see the rabbi and ask him if can be eaten, them hamburgers. And I remember to this day, that the rabbi said that he'll-- he's given permission that we can eat that. Shows you how religious, or kosher, we were. That even in depressed times we didn't touch it without a, uh, rabbi permission. Was there a, a Judenrat, or Jewish Police Force in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They weren't armed, but they were doing the dirty work for the Nazis and the supporters of the Nazis. They were doing-- and if they needed 20 Jews for some work, they went to the Jewish Judenrat, and they did-- and they said, OK, we need 20 people, or whatever they needed, or they needed money, or gold, or whatever they made the Judenrat do the dirty work.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were the members of the Judenrat chosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't tell you. I really can't remember. But I know they wore a yellow, a yellow stripe on their--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they receive special privileges?

speaker: interviewee
text: Really not. They-- they killed them just as well in the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: Refugees came from other towns into your ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they rounded up every Jew that was in the surrounding district and brought them into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they bring you news about what was happening outside of your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew what was happening because there was always somebody going to the next towns and checking. Like always came a report that the next town, [NON-ENGLISH], all the-- all the Jews were killed. So we knew. Or there was always, uh-- we had connection with the local people too. And they were telling us things. And, um-- so we knew what was going on, which town was killed, judenrein which.

speaker: interviewer
text: What contact did you have with the local population?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I said, there was no fences. So when Father went out at night to deal, bring some food, trade with the farmers, he-- he got the reports from the local people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was not Jews coming from other towns.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jews were going to other towns too. It wasn't that hard because that ghetto was not fenced in. You could get out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there curfews or anything else imposed on you when you were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So certain hours you had to be in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people tell you about the extermination camps? Did you receive knowledge of those?

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard about them. We heard, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any actions in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon, what do you mean by actions? Killing? Yeah. In, uh, '42, in May, that was the first, we called it action, that was the first killing of the Jews. And, uh, I woke up early in the morning. It was a Saturday morning. And, uh, my brother and I, we woke up. And my other brother, the big brother, he was working on field, uh. [PAUSES] It's, uh-- I couldn't explain it in English. It's, uh, a product that you burn in stoves. You take it-- it's turf from the ground, and you dry it. Anyways, he was working in that field.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he went every day, the older brother. And when we woke up in the morning, we noticed the Gestapo in town, just came in the edge of the town. So we-- As we were told, when the Gestapo comes in, there's troubles. So I ran in to scream to the people in the ghetto that the Gestapo was in. We all started running.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was running with my brother and I. And we ran-- everybody ran on his own. [PAUSES] That was in May-- I was 12-- of '42. And my brother and I, we ran. Like I said before, we knew the country well so we had no problems knowing where to go. So we ran towards the farms. And we ran by the farm close where my older brother was working. And as we ran there, we seen the German trucks are coming towards it. So we run away from there to the fields, and then to the-- far away from that, where they were digging for that-- and there they picked up all the Jewish guys that were working on the fields including, in that group, was my brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in that time, they took half, at least close to half, of the Jewish people. And they drove them out of town, and they shot-- including, in that group, was my mother, and my little sister, and my older brother that was working that time. My brother and I, we survived, and my father did. He run away. He wasn't caught. [PAUSES] Shall I continue further?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then [PAUSES] the Nazis, they tricked again. They never admitted they-- they killed the Jews. They always said they resettled them. But we knew. It was five miles out of town, they took them all, and undressed them all, and shot them all. Then the Nazis came with another trick. And they said, we won't touch nobody now. Please come back to town.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we did come back to town. And, uh, [PAUSES] and, uh, my father came, and, uh, my brother and I, we went back into the ghetto. And, uh, [PAUSES] that, uh, that ghetto stayed until August, about. And then came Juden-- pardon me, judenrein, which means all Jews have to be destroyed.

speaker: interviewee
text: But before that happened, uh, my father took me to a farm. And he told the farmer to hold me, that I will do work for him, which I did. And I was looking after the cattle on the field for that farmer. And he took my brother, Jack, or Yitzhak, to another farm. Uh-- and, uh, he worked with the other farmer doing the same thing. And father was working for some other farmer. We knew, more or less, where he was. And he knew where we were. And then, in August came judenrein, so we couldn't come back to town. That was the end of Werba and Jewish people in Werba.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just to go back, where was your mother during the first action?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she was in that group killed. I mentioned it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she-- was she in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was running also, but they caught her. See, everybody was running and they caught about half. And half managed to run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father took you to a farm outside of Werba. Did he pay the farmer?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We-- like-- father was very well-known between the farmers. And he-- I guess some of them couldn't refuse. And, uh-- and it wasn't judenrein yet, so the farmers didn't take much of a chance by employing a Jewish person. But when judenrein came, then was a problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, when judenrein came, [PAUSES] a large portion of Jews run away. And they were hiding on farms and different settlements. And the pro-Nazi sympathizers, the local people, they knew who survived, and they were hunting them. So little by little, they found quite a few of them and killed them. Me, the farmer told me that he cannot longer hold me, that I have to move. I was-- [PAUSES] that could have been end of '42, beginning of '43. [PAUSES]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to look for my father. I knew more or less-- like, I didn't have problems of the country. I knew the country. But it took me three days of sleeping on the fields. And as a miracle happened, a local person walked by, he seen me on the field, and he recognized me. He says, you must be so and so. And I said, yes. He says, stay here until tonight and I'll take you to your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as sure, he did come back at night. And my father was hiding at his place. So he took me down there. And that was a Ukrainian village. Let me put some into that before I go any farther. Our area was Ukrainian villages. Then we had three villages, Czech, Czech villages. The father always told us if you have a problem, the Czech villagers are safer to the Jews, or friendlier to the Jews, to always run to a Czech village. And the Czech villages, was three of them. They were in a radius of 20 miles. But father was in the Ukrainian village. He was hiding there. So I was with father. A few days later brother, Jack, came. So we were reunited. We were hiding in the barn, and that them people, they fed us. I don't know if father paid, maybe, in gold or something. I couldn't-- I really didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do all day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you do all day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just hide in the straw piles. Because, like I said, the local population knew who survived, and they were hunting for the Jews. They knew that father and two of us were living. They were hunting somewhere. They didn't know where we were. So we had to-- we couldn't go out. So all we did is lay in the barns, in the straw piles. Finally, things got very hot, as we used to say it. That the Nazi sympathizers were tracking closer to us. So father said we got to run. [PAUSES] So Jack and I, we took off at night. And father said-- I cannot recollect exactly where he's going to meet us, somehow.

speaker: interviewee
text: But as we walked out, Jack and I, we were hiding in that Ukrainian village, different places, straw piles. And one night we walked out, and there was two Nazi sympathizers that, uh, caught us, my brother and I. I think I told you about it, didn't I? And he said, I'm-- the Germans are close in here. I'd like to hide you. So come on, follow me. So he went in the middle between my brother and I. My brother was on the right, of his right. I was on his left. And the second guy disappeared as he-- then we walked about-- we got out of the residential area to the field, and I hear a shot, a bullet, a gunshot. So I naturally started running.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I started running, the, uh, guy chased me, maybe a quarter of a kilometer, or a mile, whatever. He hollered to his buddy. And his buddy was right in front of me, so he caught me. So when the-- the buddy of his left, and he said to me, walk this way. Then he took a shot with the gun. And the gun wouldn't go off. He tried a few times, and it wouldn't go off. Tried to fix it, and it wouldn't. So he took me to the other guy's place. And the other-- the dogs started barking, so the other guy came out, and I guess they changed guns. Then he told me, go straight for that star, for that-- in that field, which I did. And then I hear a shot. And that was on the fields. That could've been in the spring of '43. The fields were wet and I fell down. And I thought I was dead. Then he took another shot. And I laid without moving. And I thought for sure that's the way a guy is dead. And after about a few minutes he disappeared, the guy. So I got up. And like father used to tell us, if you're in trouble, try and run to the Czech village. So I run straight in the middle of the night to the straight-- to the-- pardon me- to the Ch-- Czech village. [PAUSES] And I went into a barn, and lay down, fell asleep on a straw pile. And in the morning, I wake up. And I look, I got blood all over. And I look here, and I got a hole in my shoulder in the front and the back. So I guess the bullet went through the back and came out through the front. And I guess the second bullet missed me. [PAUSES] I presumed my brother was dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was laying, when I seen the blood all over, I got panicky. And I ran out to the people, the Czech people, I knew them. Like, uh, I knew most farmers there. So I ran into the house-- or the lady was in the barn, and I approached her for some help. I said I was shot, I need a little water to wash up. And she did bring me, she fed me, she kept me for about three, four-- two, three weeks. That's guessing time, I really can't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I couldn't move my hand for awhile. But after that, after two weeks, I could move it. And she said, I would like you to go. [PAUSES] I couldn't find my father. I thought my brother is dead. So I went in the same village, that was called Sofievka, the town- ah, village.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the spelling of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sofievka-- S-O-F-I-J-E-W-I-- A approximately. And then I went at night, she gave me food, that lady, which was very nice. And she cured me a little bit, and helped me out. And I went to another barn, and I hid myself. I stayed for over a week, and I ran out of food. And this was already spring. The farmers were cutting potatoes for seed in the barn. And the lady was in the barn. But I said, I had to get up because I'm starving to death. I'll be dead from-- might as well get up.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I get up. And, uh, the lady got scared, but she recognized me, that-- who I was. And I said to her, would you like to feed me? She said, but you were just here half an hour ago and I gave you food. What did you do with it? I said, I wasn't here. And I never got any food from you. She says, I'm not dreaming. I did give you the food.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then she says, maybe it was your brother. So I says, my brother is dead. She said, well, he just left 5-10 minutes ago. I fed him. So I ran out, and I-- sure, it was my brother, Jack. And I found him on the field. [PAUSES] Then it's a long story after that. [PAUSES] Then we-- our father found us. We were hiding in different places. We went next to another Czech village, Smoliania, it's called.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-M-O-L-I-A-N-I-A, approximately. And we were hiding there. Father was with us at times. But always hiding without the farmers knowing, digging into a straw pile. And we stayed there maybe for a few months. Then things get hot, you know. They were closing in on us. They-- somehow we had a feeling, so we moved back to Sofievka again, and we hid in a different farm, in a straw pile. We dug in. And then my brother, Jack, had to meet my father in a certain farm to get some food. And there, the local Nazis, Ukrainian Nazis, caught my father. And that was the end of Father. My father hollered to Jack to run. Jack run away, came back where I was hiding and told me that they caught the father, that was, they killed the father then. There was only two of us left. And that was-- could be middle of [PAUSES] '43, then we became orphans, both of us. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Then we were hiding in different places, and lots of hardships. We hardly had a bath and [PAUSES] I guess lots of tears, and we had lots of lice. The lice were in, uh, my eyebrows, my eyelid, my-- all over the body. I would go like this, and just like-- surprising didn't get typhus or-- then, but-- but they get used to us after a while. We couldn't get rid of them because we couldn't go and have a bath. We were always without the farmer knowing, where we could go and have a little wash up but never-- and also we had nearly the same clothes all the time, never change. In the summertime, we would steal fruit from the trees. From the gardens, we would steal cucumbers, vegetables. We would eat-- one day [PAUSES] we were stealing apples. We were-- and we hear somebody's walking. And that was-- we look and there's another Jewish guy, Lazarus is his name-- which, he survived also-- and he was hiding in a fire hall, in the ceiling. And after-- so he said, you could stay with me for-- he was quite a bit older than us. So he said, you kids can stay with us. And he helped us quite a bit. He-- he fed us a little bit. And after we got into that ceiling of the fire hall, it was very small, we climbed with a ladder. And he pulled the ladder back to the top so nobody would know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we stayed with him for a few weeks, not too long. And things, I guess, got hot. What I mean by hot, they were getting closer to us, the Nazi sympathizers, the ones that were chasing the Jews. OK.
